displayName: Preservation of Evidence Letter (Personal Injury — Generic)
template:
**DOCUMENT FORMAT — PRESERVATION OF EVIDENCE LETTER (PERSONAL INJURY — GENERIC)**:
A pre-suit litigation-hold letter sent immediately upon engagement to defendants, third-party custodians, insurers, employers, premises owners, and any other entity in possession or control of evidence relating to the incident. Establishes the duty to preserve, defines the scope of preservation, and documents notice for sanctions analysis under FRCP 37(e) and applicable state spoliation doctrine. Target length: 2–3 pages.

**WHAT THIS TEMPLATE IS — AND WHAT IT IS NOT**:
This is the GENERIC / TRUNK preservation letter for state-agnostic personal-injury matters. It is NOT a litigation-hold notice circulated *internally* within a corporate party (those documents follow the Sedona Conference / Zubulake V framework but are addressed to in-house custodians — out of scope here). It is NOT a vehicle-specific preservation letter (`vehicle_preservation_letter_pi` adds 49 C.F.R. Part 563 EDR-rule language) nor a premises-specific surveillance preservation letter (`surveillance_preservation_letter_pi` adds CCTV-cycle language). For those workflows, use the dedicated siblings.

**DYNAMIC BEHAVIOR — READ documentOptions**:
- `recipientType`: `defendant_individual` (default) | `corporate_defendant` | `third_party_custodian` | `insurer` | `employer` | `government_entity`
- `subPractice`: `mva` | `premises` | `med_mal` | `product_liability` | `workers_comp_third_party` | (omit for general PI) — selects framing of the evidence inventory
- `evidenceCategories`: array — `physical_evidence`, `electronic_records`, `paper_records`, `video_surveillance`, `audio_recordings`, `mobile_devices`, `email_correspondence`, `social_media`, `cloud_repositories`, `metadata_audit_trails`
- `includeESILitigationHold`: boolean (default true) — whether to invoke FRCP 37(e) ESI-preservation duty explicitly
- `includeSanctionsWarning`: boolean (default true) — whether to cite FRCP 37(e) sanctions and Zubulake V negligence/gross-negligence framework
- `preservationDuration`: `until_close_of_litigation` (default) | `specified_date` | `until_written_release_by_counsel`
- `requestWrittenConfirmation`: boolean (default true) — request confirmation within 14 days that the hold is in place
- `includeFRCP34Notice`: boolean (default false) — flag intent to seek inspection / production under FRCP 34 (or state equivalent)

**STRUCTURE** (follow line by line):

1. Date line.
2. VIA line (bold) — `**VIA Certified Mail — Return Receipt Requested AND Email**` (default; the certified-mail receipt is critical to documenting notice for any later spoliation motion).
3. Recipient block — name; title; entity; address. For corporate recipients, address to the General Counsel or Risk Management Department.
4. Re: block (multi-line, aligned labels):
   Re:    Our Client:           [client name]
          Date of Incident:     [date of incident]
          Location of Incident: [location]
          Subject:              **Notice of Anticipated Litigation and Demand for Preservation of Evidence**
5. Greeting — `Dear [Recipient name]:` if known, else `Dear Sir or Madam:`.

**BODY SECTIONS** (in order):

### I. Notice of Representation and Anticipated Litigation
One paragraph: this office represents the client identified above with respect to personal injuries sustained on [date of incident] at [location]. Litigation is reasonably anticipated. State explicitly: 'Your duty to preserve potentially relevant evidence is triggered by this notice and arises independently under common law, the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, and applicable state law.'

### II. The Duty to Preserve
One paragraph citing the legal foundation:
- Common-law duty to preserve evidence reasonably likely to be relevant to litigation, citing **Silvestri v. General Motors Corp., 271 F.3d 583 (4th Cir. 2001)**.
- Federal duty under **Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(e)** for electronically stored information (when `includeESILitigationHold=true`).
- Issuance of a litigation hold by the recipient, citing **Zubulake v. UBS Warburg LLC, 229 F.R.D. 422 (S.D.N.Y. 2004)** ('Zubulake V'), and the **ABA Civil Discovery Standards Standard 10 (Litigation Hold)**.
- The Sedona Conference Commentary on Legal Holds: The Trigger and the Process, 11 Sedona Conf. J. 265 (2010), as the recognized framework for issuing and documenting holds.
- State-specific spoliation doctrine where applicable — defer to JURISDICTION CONTEXT (e.g., adverse-inference instruction; spoliation as independent tort in some jurisdictions).

### III. Scope of Preservation
One or more paragraphs identifying — at a minimum — the following categories. Adapt based on `subPractice` and `evidenceCategories`:
- **Physical evidence**: any object, instrumentality, or article of clothing involved in or relevant to the incident, in its present condition.
- **Electronic records (when `electronic_records` ∈ evidenceCategories)**: all electronically stored information (ESI) — including emails, text messages, instant messages, internal chats (Slack, Teams), cloud storage (Google Drive, OneDrive, SharePoint, Dropbox), databases, server logs, application logs, and backups — whether on premises servers, cloud infrastructure, employee devices, or third-party systems.
- **Paper records**: incident reports, accident reports, witness statements, employee files, training records, maintenance logs, inspection reports, work orders, invoices, contracts, policies and procedures.
- **Video surveillance (when `video_surveillance` ∈ evidenceCategories)**: all CCTV, dashcam, body-camera, and other video records bearing on the incident, including the period before and after the incident as well as the incident itself; preserved in **native format** without compression, conversion, redaction, or annotation.
- **Audio recordings (when `audio_recordings` ∈ evidenceCategories)**: any 911 calls, dispatch recordings, two-way radio, customer-service phone calls, voicemail messages, and meeting recordings.
- **Mobile devices (when `mobile_devices` ∈ evidenceCategories)**: company-issued and personally-owned mobile devices used for business purposes by witnesses, supervisors, drivers, and other potentially-relevant custodians, including text messages, call logs, application data, and location/GPS data.
- **Email correspondence (when `email_correspondence` ∈ evidenceCategories)**: all email — sent, received, deleted, archived — relating to the incident, the client, or any custodian identified during the matter.
- **Social media (when `social_media` ∈ evidenceCategories)**: corporate social-media posts, comments, and direct messages relating to the incident or the client.
- **Cloud repositories (when `cloud_repositories` ∈ evidenceCategories)**: third-party SaaS application data — Salesforce, ServiceNow, Workday, Concur, etc. — including auto-purge or retention-policy overrides.
- **Metadata and audit trails (when `metadata_audit_trails` ∈ evidenceCategories)**: original metadata, modification history, access logs, and audit trails for any document or record produced.

### IV. Custodian Identification
One paragraph: request that the recipient identify, in writing, all custodians with potentially relevant information — including current and former employees, agents, contractors, supervisors, eyewitnesses, and IT/Records Management personnel. Issue a written litigation hold to each identified custodian. Suspend any auto-delete, retention-policy purge, or routine destruction schedule that would touch the categories above.

### V. Sanctions for Spoliation [include only when includeSanctionsWarning=true]
One paragraph: failure to preserve evidence after this notice exposes the recipient to severe sanctions — including but not limited to:
- Adverse-inference jury instruction (state and federal).
- Monetary sanctions, attorneys' fees, and costs.
- Preclusion of evidence or theories.
- For ESI under Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(e): if intentional spoliation is found (loss with intent to deprive a party), dispositive sanctions including default judgment and dismissal are available.
- In some jurisdictions, spoliation may give rise to an **independent tort claim** — defer to JURISDICTION CONTEXT.
State explicitly: 'This notice removes any plausible claim of inadvertence as a defense to a spoliation finding.'

### VI. Inspection Notice [include only when includeFRCP34Notice=true]
One paragraph: this office reserves the right, under Fed. R. Civ. P. 34 and applicable state-court rules, to demand inspection, photograph, sampling, and non-destructive testing of physical evidence, premises, and equipment after suit is filed. The recipient is cautioned that any alteration of the evidence prior to such inspection may be the subject of a motion in limine and/or sanctions.

### VII. Confirmation Request and Closing [include confirmation request only when requestWrittenConfirmation=true]
One paragraph: please provide written confirmation within fourteen (14) days that (1) a litigation hold has been issued, (2) the categories above have been preserved, and (3) the auto-delete and retention-policy purge schedules have been suspended for the duration of the matter (`preservationDuration`). Direct all communications to undersigned counsel.

Closing: `Very truly yours,` (suppress when stationery signature block is applied).

**CONSTRAINTS**:
- Do NOT cite a specific state spoliation cause-of-action, jury instruction, or sanctions framework unless JURISDICTION CONTEXT supplies it — use generic 'state-specific spoliation doctrine' framing.
- Do NOT identify custodians by name unless the firm has confirmed their identity and role — use 'all custodians identified during the matter' framing.
- Do NOT include EDR / ECM / telematics-specific preservation language — use `vehicle_preservation_letter_pi` for that.
- Do NOT include CCTV-specific overwrite-cycle language — use `surveillance_preservation_letter_pi` for that.
- For any disabled `evidenceCategories` entry, suppress that bullet entirely — never emit a placeholder-only line.
- If firm stationery is applied, SUPPRESS the `Very truly yours,` closing and signature block (the stationery handles them).
- Total body text: 600–1,200 words. Target page count: 2–3 pages.
hints: Generic state-agnostic preservation-of-evidence (litigation-hold) letter. The TRUNK template — vehicle preservation letters add 49 C.F.R. Part 563 EDR language; surveillance preservation letters add CCTV overwrite-cycle language. Cite Silvestri v. GMC (4th Cir. 2001), FRCP 37(e), Zubulake V (S.D.N.Y. 2004), ABA Civ. Discovery Std. 10, and Sedona Conference Commentary on Legal Holds for the duty-to-preserve foundation. Defer state-specific spoliation tort and adverse-inference doctrine to JURISDICTION CONTEXT. Time-sensitive — send at intake. Track certified-mail receipt for sanctions documentation. 2–3 pages.

verificationSources.citation: Silvestri v. General Motors Corp., 271 F.3d 583 (4th Cir. 2001)
Fed. R. Civ. P. 37(e) (Failure to Preserve Electronically Stored Information)
Zubulake v. UBS Warburg LLC, 229 F.R.D. 422 (S.D.N.Y. 2004) (Zubulake V)
ABA Civil Discovery Standards Standard 10 (Litigation Hold)
The Sedona Conference Commentary on Legal Holds: The Trigger and the Process, 11 Sedona Conf. J. 265 (2010)

knownLimitations: Generic — does NOT include vehicle/EDR-specific preservation language. Use vehicle_preservation_letter_pi for MVA cases involving on-board diagnostics, EDR, ECM, telematics, or dashcam preservation.

Generic — does NOT include CCTV/video-overwrite-cycle language. Use surveillance_preservation_letter_pi for premises cases where 7–30 day surveillance overwrite cycles create urgent preservation timing.

State spoliation doctrine varies widely — some states recognize spoliation as an independent tort (e.g., NM, AK, NJ, OH for first-party); others limit relief to adverse-inference instruction or evidentiary sanctions. Template defers to JURISDICTION CONTEXT.
FRCP 37(e) applies to ESI in federal court; analogous state-court rules vary in scope and intent-of-deprivation thresholds.
Internal corporate litigation-hold notices (addressed to in-house custodians) are out of scope — those follow the Zubulake V / Sedona Commentary framework but are written in a different register.
Custodian-identification requests presume the recipient will cooperate; non-cooperation requires Rule 26/30(b)(6) discovery to compel.
